text: The surgeon begins the cystectomy with a peritoneal incision in the Douglas pouch, using sharp and blunt dissection to separate the seminal vesicles from the Denonvilliers fascia. The fascia is incised, and the posterior surface of the prostate is dissected from the perirectal tissue. The vas deferens on both sides are isolated and cut. Seminal vesicles are dissected from surrounding tissue up to their junction with the prostate and posterior bladder neck. The ureters are isolated, with the peritoneum over the iliac artery excised, and the ureters dissected from surrounding tissue, preserving periureteral tissue to prevent ischemia. The ureters are dissected to the uretero-vesicle junction, clipped twice, and transected, with a frozen section sent to pathology. The incision is extended alongside the lateral border of the median umbilical ligament, and the lateral wall of the bladder is separated from the pelvic side wall using sharp and blunt dissection. The umbilical artery is clipped twice at its origin and transected with ligature. The surgeon continues dissection, controlling the pedicle with repeated application of LigaSure and sharp dissection with scissors.

The surgeon dissects the retzius space anteriorly, dropping the bladder from the abdominal wall after freeing the prostate and bladder from the rectum. The Alexis port at the stoma site is visible in the upper right corner, used for safe insufflation and future urinary diversion. Periprostatic fatty tissue is dissected, exposing the dorsal venous complex. Sharp dissection of the dorsal venous complex is performed with minimal bleeding due to pneumoperitoneum pressure, followed by complete transection and vascular control using A-III-Zero monofilament suture ligature.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the prostatic apex and urethra, clipping the urethra twice before transection to prevent urine spillage. A frozen section is taken from the apical urethra. The specimen is bagged and removed through a small phantasteel incision, or alternatively, the bag is secured in the abdomen to prevent spillage.

The surgeon transfers the left ureter beneath the colon to the right side and marks a segment of ileum approximately twenty centimeters from the ileocecal valve. The ileum is divided with staplers, and continuity is restored by a side-to-side anastomosis using two fires of sixty millimeter staplers. The left ureter is opened and spatulated, followed by an incision at the lateral border of the ileum for uretero-ileal anastomosis. A four-oh PDS suture is used to fix the ureter to the ileal wall. The distal end of the isolated ileal segment is brought out using the Alexis port, and a metal suction cannula is inserted into the ileum. A ureter catheter loaded on a guide wire is inserted into the ureter, allowing it to exit through the ileal stoma without additional punctures. The ureteral catheter is fixed to the ileum with A-V-O rapid vicryl suture.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis of the ureters to the ileum using continuous sutures with 4-0 PDS on both sides. The distal end of the ileum is then exteriorized through the Alexis port and matured into a stoma. This technique minimizes manipulation and reduces the risk of urine leakage by avoiding additional punctures.
